name: Yodlee (Envestnet) API
what_it_does: Yodlee (owned by Envestnet) is an enterprise financial data aggregation platform that connects to thousands of financial institutions to retrieve account balances, transaction history, investment holdings, and net worth data. It serves banks, wealth management firms, and financial applications.
use_cases: Aggregating financial data across a user's bank, brokerage, and credit accounts
Net worth and cash flow analysis for wealth management platforms
Transaction categorization and enrichment for personal finance apps
Financial wellness scoring and advisory applications
Data feeds for enterprise financial planning systems
not_for: Startups and small applications (enterprise pricing and lengthy onboarding)
Simple bank balance verification (Plaid is more accessible)
Real-time payment initiation (primarily a data aggregation platform)
best_when: Building enterprise financial applications requiring broad institution coverage, investment data, or wealth management features not available in Plaid.
avoid_when: You need quick developer access, transparent pricing, or are building a consumer app — Plaid's developer experience is significantly better.

auth.notes: cobrandName and cobrandPassword for application-level auth, then loginName and userToken for user-level context. JWT-based auth available in newer API versions. Complex multi-level auth model (cobrand session + user session) is a notable friction point for agents.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free sandbox available for development and testing with simulated accounts
pricing.paid_tiers: Enterprise pricing — custom contracts only. No public pricing. Costs are typically per-user per-month, negotiated based on volume and features.
pricing.notes: Pricing is opaque and enterprise-only. No self-serve paid tier. Sales process required. Typically positioned at larger financial institutions and established fintechs.

performance.rate_limits: Rate limits defined per contract. Not publicly documented. Sandbox limits are lower than production.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses include errorCode, errorMessage, and referenceCode fields. Error codes are somewhat documented but require cross-referencing with developer portal documentation. Error messages can be generic. Institution-specific errors are common and less well-documented.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency key mechanism. API is primarily read-oriented which reduces risk, but lack of idempotency on write operations (user registration, account linking) requires agents to check existing state.
known_agent_gotchas: Two-level auth model (cobrand session + user session) requires agents to manage and refresh two token types
Session tokens expire and require periodic refresh — agents must handle session expiry gracefully
Initial account linking requires a user-interactive FastLink widget (similar to Plaid Link)
Data refresh is asynchronous — agents must poll or use webhooks to know when fresh data is available
Institution coverage varies — some institutions require screen-scraping which is fragile
Sandbox data is synthetic and may not reflect production institution-specific quirks
Documentation is fragmented across developer portal, API reference, and support knowledge base
Rate limits are not communicated in response headers — agents learn limits through failures

score_rationale: Yodlee's enterprise focus and complex two-level auth model make it significantly harder for agents to work with than Plaid. The combination of session token management, asynchronous data refresh, user-interactive account linking, and opaque rate limits creates substantial operational overhead. Documentation quality is lower than competitors. The platform shines for enterprise wealth management use cases but is a poor fit for autonomous agent workflows that require predictable, stateless API interactions.
